mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage policies as code for security, compliance, and governance. It is particularly useful for platform engineering teams looking to enforce policies across their Kubernetes clusters. Key trade-offs include the need for a learning curve to effectively write policies and the potential performance impact on the cluster due to policy evaluations.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Kyverno repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace, creating the namespace if it doesn't exist.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno || true

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version v1.17.1

```

> Creates the namespace if it doesn't already exist.
> Installs Kyverno with the specified version.
commands: helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version v1.17.1

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Kyverno pods are running successfully.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Lists all pods in the kyverno namespace to verify their status.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This command creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's capabilities.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-label
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-label
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

```

> Creates a policy that requires a label on Pods.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-label
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-label
      match:
        resources:
          kinds:
            - Pod
      validate:
        message: "Label 'app' is required."
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              app: ?
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This command removes Kyverno from the cluster. Be aware that this will delete all policies and associated resources managed by Kyverno.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstalls the Kyverno release.
> Deletes the Kyverno namespace, which may leave orphaned resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusterpolicies -o yaml > clusterpolicies-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version v1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backs up existing cluster policies.
> Upgrades Kyverno to the specified version.
> Verifies that the new pods are running.
commands: helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version v1.17.1

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'validation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that a policy is enforcing a rule that is not being met by the resource.

**Fix:**
Check the policy definition and ensure the resource meets the specified requirements.

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in the policies or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Policies not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This could be due to the policies being in a 'failed' state.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl get clusterpolicies' to check the status and correct any issues.

troubleshooting.title: Unable to create resources due to policy violations
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the resource does not comply with the existing policies.

**Fix:**
Review the policies and adjust the resource definitions accordingly.

versionNotes.changes: Improved support for validating custom resources, added new policy templates.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with a status of 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno'.

    - name: check-label

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources.
